---
title: "Deployment"
description: "How Polydelve ships: rolling deploys, AWS topology, and ETL scheduling."
---

## Deploy strategy

Polydelve uses a **rolling deploy with `min_healthy_percent=0`**. There are no active users, so zero-downtime guarantees (canary, blue-green) add complexity with no benefit. Rolling with min_healthy=0 lets ECS stop the old task immediately and start the new one — fastest possible iteration cycle.

Canary and blue-green deploys are explicitly out of scope for this project.

## AWS topology

```
Internet
  └── CloudFront (CDN / TLS termination)
        └── Backend API (ECS Fargate — Dockerized)
              └── RDS Postgres
```

- **CloudFront** — serves the frontend (S3 origin) and proxies `/api/*` to the backend.
- **Backend API** — runs the FastAPI app inside a Docker container on ECS Fargate.
- **RDS Postgres** — primary datastore; Alembic manages schema migrations.

Terraform manages provisioning. Full reference: [Infrastructure](/docs/build/infrastructure).

## Shipping a backend release

1. **Build image** from `backend/Dockerfile`:

   ```bash
   docker build -t polydelve-backend:$TAG ./backend
   ```

2. **Push** to ECR (or your configured registry):

   ```bash
   docker push $ECR_REPO/polydelve-backend:$TAG
   ```

3. **Update ECS service** — force a new deployment so ECS pulls the new image:

   ```bash
   aws ecs update-service \
     --cluster polydelve \
     --service backend \
     --force-new-deployment
   ```

   ECS performs the rolling update: stops the existing task (min_healthy=0), starts the new task with the updated image.

4. **Run migrations** if the release includes schema changes:

   ```bash
   alembic upgrade head
   ```

   Run migrations against the live RDS instance before or immediately after the new task starts, depending on whether the change is backward-compatible.

ECS task definition updates (new image tag, env vars, resource limits) are managed via Terraform. See [Infrastructure](/docs/build/infrastructure) for the full resource reference.

## ETL job scheduling

ETL jobs run via `python -m etl <job>` (see `backend/etl/run.py`). The primary scheduler is **EventBridge Scheduler** — a Fargate task runs on a `rate(2 hours)` schedule executing the `hourly` composite job. Individual jobs can also be triggered manually or via CI.

| Cadence | Command | What it does |
|---------|---------|--------------|
| Every 2 hours (EventBridge) | `hourly` composite | EPSS refresh + news update + MAL advisories |
| On-demand | `make etl-epss` | Refresh EPSS scores from BigQuery; resolve open contracts |
| On-demand | `make etl-news` | Fetch security news, extract entities, regenerate featured contracts |
| On-demand | `make build-cve-history` | Bulk-load OSV CVE history for all packages |
| On-demand | `make enrich-packages` | Update download stats and risk scores |

See [Data Pipeline](/docs/build/data-pipeline) for the full ETL architecture and bootstrap order.

## Infrastructure

Terraform provisions and manages all AWS resources (CloudFront, ECS, RDS, ECR, IAM, DNS, ACM). Full reference: [Infrastructure](/docs/build/infrastructure).
